fuzzy_prd:
Our package management platform needs a robust version intelligence engine that enables developers to work with software release strings and the rules that govern compatibility between them. The engine must handle the full spectrum of version formats found in the wild — numeric releases, pre-release markers, date-stamped builds, development branch names, and aliased identifiers — and convert any recognized input into a single canonical comparable form that makes ordering and equality checks reliable and consistent.

Beyond normalization, the engine must support filtering a list of candidate releases against a range expression to find what is acceptable, sorting releases from oldest to newest or newest to oldest, and evaluating whether two arbitrary range rules can ever be satisfied by the same release at the same time. Range expressions themselves can be constructed from exact pins, directional comparators, wildcard slots, tilde shorthand, caret shorthand, and inclusive hyphen spans, and multiple clauses can be combined with AND or OR semantics.

Error cases must be surfaced as neutral, language-agnostic category codes — never as raw exception messages — so that the surrounding tooling remains decoupled from implementation details. The system should follow the separation logic described in the internal "stability rules" module and the "branch normalization" pathway. All behavior must be exercised through a thin adapter layer that routes JSON commands to the core engine and prints plain-text results.

A couple follow-ups from the team questions. On normalization, the canonical result always comes out as exactly four dot-separated numeric segments, so think '1.0.0.0', with any missing pieces padded using '.0', and the returned text is that four-segment string plus the newline character '\n'. If something can’t be normalized, the output is exactly 'error=invalid_version\n' and that’s all we expose. No exception type, no stack trace. The cases that should land there include empty string, non-numeric junk like 'a', unknown stability words like 'meh', more than four numeric segments, arbitrary non-dev names, and build metadata with whitespace. Also, the branch aliases 'master', 'trunk', and 'default', whether they show up bare or with a 'dev-' prefix, all normalize to '9999999-dev' in both normalize() and normalizeBranch().

On branch matching, the important default is that without the 'compareBranches' flag, or if it’s false or just absent, anything starting with 'dev-' is NEVER compatible with a numeric bound, so constraintMatch always returns 'false\n'. Two dev branches only line up under '==' if their full names are identical. If compareBranches: true is set, then a dev branch is treated as ordering BELOW any numeric version, which means a '<' upper bound accepts it and a '>' lower bound rejects it.

A couple range details were worth pinning down too. For tilde, if there’s no explicit stability, the lower bound gets a '-dev' suffix, so '~1.2' becomes '[>= 1.2.0.0-dev < 2.0.0.0-dev]'. The upper bound increments the segment one position more significant than the last specified digit. So '~1' and '~1.0' both yield '[>= 1.0.0.0-dev < 2.0.0.0-dev]', and '~1.2.3' yields '[>= 1.2.3.0-dev < 1.3.0.0-dev]'. For a hyphen range 'A - B', if B is partial, meaning 1 or 2 segments like '2' or '2.1', the upper side is exclusive and you increment the last supplied segment. If B is complete, meaning 3 or 4 segments like '2.3.4' or '2.3.4.5', the upper side is inclusive at the fully normalized B. The examples to keep using are '1 - 2' -> '[>= 1.0.0.0-dev < 3.0.0.0-dev]' and '1.2.3 - 2.3.4.5' -> '[>= 1.2.3.0-dev <= 2.3.4.5]'.

And for sorting behavior, in ascending sort with the 'sort' action, a pre-release like '2.4.0-alpha' comes BEFORE the corresponding stable '2.4.0'. In descending 'rsort', that flips so stable comes before pre-release. If two items compare equal, keep their original relative order from input, so this stays a stable sort, and the output should preserve the original input string form of each version.

Last thing, the recognized stability words and shorthand still come from the same place: the parseStability() static method in src/VersionParser.php and the $modifierRegex / $stabilities private static fields that define recognized stability words (stable, RC, beta, alpha, dev) and their shorthands.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: public function normalize(string $version, string $fullVersion = null): string

trigger_keywords: x or star in branch name
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In normalizeBranch(), wildcard segments written as 'x', 'X', or '*' (and missing trailing segments) are replaced with the integer '9999999'. For example, 'v1.x' normalizes to '1.9999999.9999999.9999999-dev'. Note: this is '9999999' (7 nines), not '9999' or '99999999'.

parseNumericAliasPrefix() returns the string 'false\n' (printed to stdout) when the branch is not in numeric form (e.g. 'dev-develop', 'dev-master'). When it is numeric (e.g. '1.0.x-dev'), it returns the dotted numeric prefix terminated by a dot and a newline, e.g. '1.0.\n'.

parseStability() returns exactly one of five lowercase strings: 'stable', 'RC', 'beta', 'alpha', 'dev'. Note 'RC' is uppercase; all others are lowercase. Shorthands: 'b' -> 'beta', 'a' -> 'alpha', 'rc' -> 'RC'. Patch markers 'p'/'pl'/'patch' return 'stable'. A 'dev-' prefix or '-dev' suffix (including '#reference' fragments) returns 'dev'.

A wildcard-only constraint expression ('*', '*.*', or 'x') parses to an EmptyConstraint whose string rendering is exactly '[]'. The output line is '[]\n'. This represents the match-anything (no restriction) constraint.

trigger_keywords: how constraints are rendered as string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A single bound renders as '<operator> <normalized-version>' (e.g. '>= 2.0.0.0-dev'). A conjunctive (AND) set renders as '[<bound1> <bound2> ...]' with a single space between members. A disjunctive (OR) set renders as '[<group1> || <group2>]' with ' || ' (space-pipe-pipe-space) between members. The whole output is followed by '\n'.

When a constraint expression is malformed, output is exactly 'error=invalid_constraint\n'. Triggers include: empty string, unknown stability word (e.g. '1.0.0-meh'), the '~>' operator, stray '#reference' on a non-dev version, doubled separators, empty disjunction alternatives.

When constraintMatch receives an unsupported operator, output is two lines: 'error=invalid_operator
' followed by 'operator=<the-offending-operator>
'. For example, operator 'invalid' produces 'error=invalid_operator
operator=invalid
'. Supported operators are: '=', '==', '<', '<=', '>', '>=', '<>', '!='.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The normalizeBranch() method in src/VersionParser.php, which handles master/trunk/default names mapping to '9999999-dev', numeric branch patterns with wildcard segments replaced by 9999999, and arbitrary names prefixed with 'dev-'.

oracle_data.fallback_response: This question does not match a documented constraint. Refer to the dispatcher (bin/RcbDispatcher.php) for the exact action routing and to src/VersionParser.php, src/Semver.php, src/Comparator.php, and src/Constraint/ for core logic. All boolean outputs are lowercase 'true' or 'false' followed by a newline; list outputs are one item per line each followed by a newline; error outputs follow the 'error=<category>' format.

feature1_satisfies.description: Decide whether a single concrete version satisfies a constraint expression. The version and the constraint expression are both supplied as strings; the expression may use exact, comparator, wildcard, tilde, caret, hyphen-range, and OR-combined forms. The result reports whether the version falls inside the set described by the expression.

feature2_satisfied_by.description: Filter a list of candidate versions, keeping only those that satisfy a constraint expression. The kept versions are returned in their original order with their original string form preserved; non-matching versions are removed.

feature3_1_sort_ascending.description: Sort a list of version strings from lowest to highest precedence. Pre-release versions order before their corresponding stable release, and equal versions keep their original relative order. The original version strings are preserved in the output.

feature3_2_sort_descending.description: Sort a list of version strings from highest to lowest precedence. A stable release orders before its pre-releases, and equal versions keep their original relative order. The original version strings are preserved in the output.

feature4_compare.description: Compare two version strings using a relational operator and report whether the relation holds. Supported operators are greater-than, greater-or-equal, less-than, less-or-equal, equal (single or double equals), and not-equal (two spellings).

feature5_1_normalize.description: Normalize a version string into a canonical four-segment numeric form suitable for comparison. A leading "v" is stripped, missing numeric segments are padded with zeros, stability suffixes (alpha/beta/RC/patch, with optional shorthand) are expanded and canonicalized, build metadata after "+" is dropped, master-like branch names map to a high synthetic version, date-based versions are recognized, and aliasing ("X as Y") keeps the source side.

feature5_2_normalize_invalid.description: Reject version strings that cannot be normalized. Empty input, non-numeric junk, unknown stability words, too many numeric segments, non-dev arbitrary names, and build metadata containing whitespace are all invalid and produce a neutral error category instead of a normalized value.

feature6_normalize_branch.description: Normalize a branch name into a comparable version form. Numeric branch names (with optional wildcard segments using x, X, or *) expand to a four-segment form where wildcard/missing segments become a high synthetic number followed by a development suffix; master-like names map to a high synthetic version; any other arbitrary name is prefixed to mark it as a development branch.

feature7_parse_stability.description: Determine the stability level of a version string. The result is one of stable, RC, beta, alpha, or dev. A trailing or leading dev marker (including dev- prefixes and -dev suffixes and reference fragments after "#") yields dev; recognized stability words and their shorthands map to their level; patch-level markers are still considered stable; anything else is stable.

feature8_numeric_alias_prefix.description: Extract the numeric prefix from a development branch name when it is in numeric form, suitable for use as a version-alias prefix. A numeric branch ending in -dev (with an optional .x segment) yields its dotted numeric prefix terminated by a dot; a non-numeric development branch yields a sentinel indicating no numeric prefix exists.

feature9_1_constraint_simple.description: Parse a single, simple constraint expression into a normalized internal representation, rendered as a text form. A bare version means exact equality; comparator-prefixed versions (=, ==, <>, !=, >, >=, <, <=) keep that operator. Open-ended lower bounds (< and >=) attach a development suffix unless an explicit stability is present, and a wildcard-only expression matches everything. The rendered form shows the operator followed by the normalized four-segment version, or square brackets for the match-anything case.

feature9_2_constraint_wildcard.description: Parse a wildcard ("x", "X", or "*") constraint into a half-open version range. A wildcard at some position becomes a lower bound (inclusive, with a development suffix) and an upper bound (exclusive) where the segment just left of the wildcard is incremented. A wildcard at the most significant position (major) yields only the exclusive upper bound. The rendered form is the bracketed pair of bounds, or the single upper bound when there is no lower bound.

feature9_3_constraint_tilde.description: Parse a tilde constraint into a half-open range allowing updates of the last specified digit. The lower bound is inclusive (with a development suffix unless an explicit stability is given); the upper bound is exclusive and increments the segment one position more significant than the last specified one. An explicit stability suffix is preserved in the lower bound.

feature9_4_constraint_caret.description: Parse a caret constraint into a half-open range that allows changes which do not modify the left-most non-zero segment of the version. The lower bound is the inclusive given version (with a development suffix when no explicit stability is present); the upper bound is exclusive and increments the left-most non-zero segment, treating leading-zero versions accordingly (e.g. 0.x allows the rest of the 0.x line, 0.0.x only patch updates).

feature9_5_constraint_hyphen.description: Parse an inclusive hyphen range "A - B" into a pair of bounds. The lower bound is the normalized A (inclusive, with a development suffix when no explicit stability is given). If B is a partial version, the upper bound is exclusive and bumps the last supplied segment of B; if B is a complete version, the upper bound is inclusive at B.

feature9_6_constraint_multi.description: Parse expressions combining several constraints. Comma- or space-separated constraints form a conjunctive (all-must-hold) group; the pipe "|" or "||" forms a disjunctive (any-may-hold) group, and disjunction has lower precedence than conjunction. A trailing per-constraint stability flag (@stable, @dev) adjusts that constraint. The rendered form nests bracketed groups, using a space between conjunctive members and " || " between disjunctive members.

feature9_7_constraint_invalid.description: Reject malformed constraint expressions. Empty input, an unknown stability word, doubled or stray separators, an empty disjunction, the mistaken "~>" operator, and a stray reference fragment "#..." on a non-dev version all fail to parse and produce a neutral error category.

feature10_1_match_single.description: Decide whether a required constraint and a provided constraint are mutually satisfiable, i.e. whether some version exists that meets both. Each side is a single operator/version pair (versions may be numeric or named development branches). Two development branches are only compatible when equal under equality operators; a development branch and a numeric version are treated as incompatible.

feature10_2_match_multi.description: Decide satisfiability when one or both sides bundle several constraints. A conjunctive set is satisfied only if every member is; a disjunctive set if any member is. Either side may itself be a set, allowing a range to be checked against a single version or against another range.

feature10_3_match_branches.description: Decide satisfiability between a numeric comparison constraint and a named development branch, with an optional flag that enables treating development branches as comparable. Without the flag, a development branch is never compatible with a numeric bound. With the flag enabled, a development branch is treated as ordering below any numeric version, so a strict upper bound accepts it while a strict lower bound does not.

feature10_4_match_invalid_operator.description: Reject construction of a comparison constraint whose operator is not one of the supported relational operators. An unsupported operator string produces a neutral error category together with the offending operator, rather than a match result.
